---
title: "CSC3107 Tidyverse Lab: Report Prose Snippets"
format: html
---

## Pre-AI predictions

Before using AI support, we expected poultry and pig meat to dominate the production-per-capita results because they are common everyday meats and can usually be scaled faster than cattle or sheep/goat production. We also expected Singapore to show low domestic meat production per resident because of its limited agricultural land, while smaller-population countries such as Brunei Darussalam might appear high in per-capita plots when production is divided by population. For Cambodia, we expected the mutton/goat series to need checking because repeated exact zeros across all years looked more like a reporting issue than a measured absence of production.

## Why sheet X.14 is not tidy

Sheet `X.14` is a presentation table, not a tidy dataset. First, years are stored as section rows such as `2021`, `2020`, `2019`, and `2018` instead of as values in a single `year` column. Second, countries are spread across many columns, so the country variable is encoded in headers rather than in one column. Third, the row labels mix real animal categories with grouping and subtotal rows such as `Livestock production`, `Poultry production`, and `Total meat production`. The sheet also contains title, unit, source, and note rows inside the same grid as the data. Because one observation is split across several row and column positions, the sheet must be reshaped before analysis.

## Cambodia mutton/goat zeros changed to NA

The Cambodia mutton/goat values were changed from `0` to `NA` because the zeros are not reliable enough to treat as confirmed production measurements. In the raw sheet, zeros are also used in structural grouping rows, so zero is not consistently a numeric observation. Cambodia has exact zeros for mutton/goat across every year while other meat categories are non-zero, which suggests missing or unavailable reporting rather than proven absence. Keeping these as true zeros would create artificially low per-capita values and could bias comparisons, so `NA` is the more conservative cleaning choice. If a source later verifies these as true zero production, they should be recoded back to `0`.

## Plot comment

The per-capita plot should be read as domestic meat production per resident, not direct meat consumption, because imports and exports are not included. A country can therefore show low production per person even if residents consume imported meat. Because the y-axis is log-scaled, the interpretation should use the calculated `meat_per_cap` values rather than judging visual slope alone. Across the available animal categories, poultry and pig production explain most of the visible increases and decreases, which matches broader meat-market evidence from the OECD-FAO Agricultural Outlook 2025-2034 linking shifts toward poultry and pigmeat to stronger productivity gains and lower relative prices for non-ruminant meats.[^oecd-fao-meat] African swine fever (ASF) is also a major regional factor for pig production because FAO describes East and Southeast Asia as highly exposed to ASF due to large pig populations and many low-biosecurity smallholder systems.[^fao-asf]

Country-level increments and decrements from 2018 to 2021 show different patterns and different reasons. Brunei Darussalam has the largest increase, rising from about 59.1 to 83.6 kg/person, mainly because poultry rises from 57.2 to 73.5 kg/person and beef-and-buffalo rises from 1.6 to 9.9 kg/person. Brunei sources link this to more farm land and poultry houses, processed-chicken demand, and more local slaughter when frozen imports were constrained.[^brunei-chicken] Cambodia is almost flat, moving from 11.3 to 11.7 kg/person; pig increases, beef-and-buffalo decreases, and mutton/goat is not interpreted because those values were corrected to `NA`. FAO's Cambodia ASF reporting supports a post-ASF recovery explanation for the pig movement, but not a strong claim for the whole total.[^cambodia-asf] Indonesia increases from 13.6 to 17.6 kg/person, mainly because poultry rises from 9.8 to 14.3 kg/person; USDA reporting on Indonesia links the 2020-2021 poultry environment to COVID-era demand pressure, weaker purchasing power, and feed dependence.[^indonesia-feed] Lao PDR shows a steady increase from 22.3 to 28.5 kg/person, with pig, poultry, and beef-and-buffalo all rising; this is consistent with reports of livestock-sector expansion and rising inventories.[^lao-livestock]

Malaysia decreases from about 65.3 to 58.0 kg/person because poultry falls from 57.8 to 49.9 kg/person, even though pig increases slightly. DOSM and finance-ministry sources point to COVID-era production constraints, labour shortages, and imported feed-cost pressure as plausible reasons.[^malaysia-dosm][^malaysia-feed] Myanmar has the largest decrease, falling from 65.5 to 20.2 kg/person, with the sharpest drop between 2020 and 2021; World Bank and USDA sources link that period to the coup, COVID-19, logistics disruption, feed-mill labour shortages, and lower feed production.[^myanmar-worldbank][^myanmar-usda] The Philippines also decreases steadily, from 34.3 to 25.1 kg/person, mainly because pig falls from 17.7 to 10.8 kg/person; PSA and Department of Agriculture sources strongly link this to ASF, movement restrictions, feed, and shipping issues.[^philippines-psa][^philippines-da] Singapore changes only slightly, from 22.2 to 21.3 kg/person, because poultry decreases while pig increases; its low domestic production is structural because Singapore imports more than 90% of its food and has very limited land for food production.[^mse-food][^sfa-stats] Thailand is stable with a small increase from 43.2 to 44.9 kg/person, mainly from poultry; USDA sources describe a poultry sector supported by domestic consumption and exports but constrained by COVID processing disruptions, weak tourism/food-service demand, high feed costs, and later ASF-related pork disruption.[^thailand-poultry][^thailand-pork] Viet Nam decreases from 54.5 to 47.3 kg/person because pig falls strongly, even though poultry increases; official statistics and USDA reports point to ASF damage in 2019, uneven swine recovery, and poultry expansion as an alternative protein source.[^vietnam-nso][^vietnam-usda]

[^oecd-fao-meat]: OECD and FAO. "Meat." *OECD-FAO Agricultural Outlook 2025-2034*. https://www.oecd.org/en/publications/oecd-fao-agricultural-outlook-2025-2034_601276cd-en/full-report/meat_5462e384.html

[^fao-asf]: FAO ECTAD Asia and the Pacific. "African swine fever." https://www.fao.org/ectad/asiapacific/our-focus/african-swine-fever/en

[^brunei-chicken]: Biz Brunei. "Local chicken supply up 25% in first half of 2021." https://www.bizbrunei.com/2021/08/local-chicken-supply-up-25-in-first-half-of-2021/

[^cambodia-asf]: FAO Cambodia. "FAO strengthens field capacities of Cambodia's officials to fight African swine fever (ASF)." https://www.fao.org/cambodia/news/detail/FAO-strengthens-field-capacities-of-Cambodia-s-officials-to-fight-African-swine-fever-%28ASF%29-/en

[^indonesia-feed]: USDA Foreign Agricultural Service. *Grain and Feed Annual: Indonesia, 2021*. https://apps.fas.usda.gov/newgainapi/api/Report/DownloadReportByFileName?fileName=Grain+and+Feed+Annual_Jakarta_Indonesia_03-27-2021.pdf

[^lao-livestock]: International Labour Organization. *Commercializing livestock production in Lao PDR*. https://www.ilo.org/sites/default/files/2024-09/PDR_Livestock_C_18_FINAL_WEB.pdf

[^malaysia-dosm]: Department of Statistics Malaysia. "Selected Agricultural Indicators, Malaysia, 2021." https://www.dosm.gov.my/portal-main/release-content/selected-agricultural-indicators-malaysia-2021

[^malaysia-feed]: Ministry of Finance Malaysia. "MOF to study best method to stabilise chicken prices." https://www.mof.gov.my/portal/en/news/press-citations/mof-to-study-best-method-to-stabilise-chicken-prices

[^myanmar-worldbank]: World Bank. "Myanmar Economic Monitor January 2022: Contending with Constraints." https://www.worldbank.org/en/country/myanmar/publication/myanmar-economic-monitor-january-2022-economic-activity-in-myanmar-to-remain-at-low-levels-with-the-overall-outlook-blea

[^myanmar-usda]: USDA Foreign Agricultural Service. *Burma - Military Coup Continues to Hamper Agricultural Trade*. https://apps.fas.usda.gov/newgainapi/api/Report/DownloadReportByFileName?fileName=Burma+-+Military+Coup+Continues+to+Hamper+Agricultural+Trade_Rangoon_Burma+-+Union+of_04-12-2021.pdf

[^philippines-psa]: Philippine Statistics Authority. *Swine Situation Report, January-March 2021*. https://psa.gov.ph/system/files/livestock-poultry/6_%255BONSrev-cleared%255D%2520170521%2520SR_Q1%25202021%2520Swine%2520Situation%2520Report_signed.pdf

[^philippines-da]: Department of Agriculture, Philippines. "OneDA Family 2021 Yearender: Propping up corn production, controlling ASF to revive livestock, poultry sector." https://www.da.gov.ph/oneda-family-2021-yearender-propping-up-corn-production-controlling-asf-to-revive-livestock-poultry-sector/

[^mse-food]: Ministry of Sustainability and the Environment. "Food." https://www.mse.gov.sg/policies/food

[^sfa-stats]: Singapore Food Agency. *Singapore Food Statistics 2021*. https://www.sfa.gov.sg/docs/default-source/publication/sg-food-statistics/singapore-food-statistics-2021.pdf

[^thailand-poultry]: USDA Foreign Agricultural Service. *Poultry and Products Annual: Thailand, 2021*. https://apps.fas.usda.gov/newgainapi/api/Report/DownloadReportByFileName?fileName=Poultry+and+Products+Annual_Bangkok_Thailand_09-01-2021.pdf

[^thailand-pork]: USDA Foreign Agricultural Service. *Thai Pork Supplies Disrupted by ASF*. https://apps.fas.usda.gov/newgainapi/api/Report/DownloadReportByFileName?fileName=Thai+Pork+Supplies+Disrupted+by+ASF_Bangkok_Thailand_01-14-2022.pdf

[^vietnam-nso]: National Statistics Office of Viet Nam. "Livestock of pig has recovered." https://www.nso.gov.vn/en/data-and-statistics/2021/04/livestock-of-pig-has-recovered/

[^vietnam-usda]: USDA Foreign Agricultural Service. *Vietnam African Swine Fever Update, 2021*. https://apps.fas.usda.gov/newgainapi/api/Report/DownloadReportByFileName?fileName=Vietnam+African+Swine+Fever+Update_Hanoi_Vietnam_05-08-2021.pdf

## AI reflection draft

AI was used through ChatGPT Desktop App as a support tool for drafting concise prose, checking whether the cleaning decisions were clearly justified, and improving the wording of the plot interpretation. The final decisions remained the group's responsibility: we checked the AI suggestions against the workbook, cleaned CSV outputs, and our tidyverse code before including them.

## Roles and contributions

- `Benjamin` - Data import and tidying: read the Excel workbook, selected the relevant sheets, reshaped the data into tidy form, and documented cleaning assumptions.
- `MeiYuen` - Data validation: checked row counts, units, country names, years, and the Cambodia mutton/goat missing-value decision against the raw sheet.
- `Joshua` - Visualisation and analysis: built the plots, selected readable labels/scales, and summarised the main production-per-capita patterns.
- `Dannon` - Constructive critic and reporter: reviewed whether the story matched the evidence, wrote the prose sections, and flagged limitations in the interpretation.
- `Joshua` - Packaging and submission lead: rendered the final QMD, checked file paths and dependencies, and assembled the final submission files.

## Final packaging checklist

- [ ] Final `.qmd` renders without errors.
- [ ] Rendered `.html` or required output file is included.
- [ ] Workbook and any cleaned CSV files needed for reproducibility are included.
- [ ] Code uses relative paths, not absolute local paths.
- [ ] Figures have clear titles, labels, units, and legends.
- [ ] Cambodia mutton/goat `NA` decision is explained in the report.
- [ ] Plot comment includes the OECD-FAO source and does not overclaim consumption from production data.
- [ ] AI reflection is completed with the actual group workflow.
- [x] Role/contribution list has real member names.
- [ ] Submission archive follows the module naming instructions and excludes unnecessary cache/temp files.
